checks.a_analysis: The output text faithfully reflects the content of the source document without introducing invented or hallucinated paragraphs. The headings, table content, and body text are all derived directly from the source material.
No phone numbers, email addresses, or URLs were introduced that do not exist in the source. The output correctly reflects the attendance status markers (X) and names without adding contact details.
All three pages of the source document are represented in the output. While a significant portion of text is missing within a section on page 3, the section itself is present as a heading/item.

The Attendees table, March Minutes approval table, and NG911 Infographic approval table all contain the expected names and data rows. The Attendees table combined side-by-side source tables into a single 6-column structure, but no material data rows were lost.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: There is a major omission in Agenda Item 10 ('Community College'). The source document contains three substantial sub-points (a, b, and c) totaling over 200 words, including details on Partnerships, Cape Fear Community College Training, and Be Rail Safe Training. The output JSON truncated the content for this item immediately after the header.
checks.b_evidence_source: 10. Community College – a) Partnerships – Ms. Turbeville reviewed a meeting that she and Mr. Greene had with Tracy McPherson... b) Cape Fear Community College Telecommunicator Training – Ms. Turbeville shared with committee Cape Fear Community College is offering free NENA and APCO training... c) Be Rail Safe Training for Telecommunicators- Ms. Turbeville welcomed guests Roger Smock and Ike Avery with NC DOT Be Rail Safe Program.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content generally preserves the meaning of the source. The metadata title contains an incorrect year (2022 instead of 2021), but this is considered an acceptable AI-generated metadata field given the correct date is present in the document body. Some item numbers (8-11) are missing in the list formatting, but the textual content for items 8, 9, and 11 is accurate.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output against the source document and found no instances of duplicated content. Every paragraph and section title appears only once in the JSON, matching its occurrence in the source PDF.

The heading hierarchy is generally preserved, starting with H1 and progressing logically to H2 and H3 for the title sections. Although some numbered section titles (like items 4-7) are rendered as paragraphs rather than headings, this falls under acceptable adjustments for normalization and flat document structures as specified in the guidelines.

The table for Meeting Attendees on page 1 is structurally corrupted in the output. The source document features a complex layout where the 'Staff' section is effectively a separate 3-column table or sub-section below the 6-column 'Committee Members' and 'Guests' tables. In the JSON, the 'Staff' header and data rows are appended to the first 6-column table, but they only contain 3 cells each. This results in a table with inconsistent column counts per row, which is a structural failure.
checks.b_evidence_source: The Staff table on Page 1 starts with a header row 'Staff | Phone | Not in Attendance' situated below the primary 6-column attendee table.
checks.b_evidence_output: The Staff row is included in the same table object (reading_order 7) but only has 3 cells: [{"text": "Staff", ...}, {"text": "Phone", ...}, {"text": "Not in Attendance", ...}]

checks.a_analysis: The reading order in the output logically follows the source PDF from top to bottom. Despite content omissions in lists (addressed in S5), the sequence of information presented matches the original document's intended flow.

Lists are converted incorrectly in two ways. First, all numbered lists (e.g., Agenda items 1-3 on page 1, and minutes items 4-11 on pages 2-3) lose their numbering and are converted to unordered (bulleted) lists. Second, there is a significant failure in list nesting and completeness: item 10 on page 3 ('Community College –') is missing its three sub-items (a, b, and c) entirely from the output.
checks.b_evidence_source:
10. Community College –
 a) Partnerships – ...
 b) Cape Fear Community College ...
 c) Be Rail Safe Training ...

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any clickable hyperlinks or URLs. The output correctly reflects this and has not injected any fabricated links.

checks.a_analysis: The agency logo (NC DIT 911 Board) has been displaced. In the source PDF, it appears at the top left of the first page as part of the document header. In the HTML output, the logo is placed between Agenda Item 3 and Agenda Item 4. While the relative order of the textual content is preserved, moving a header branding element to the middle of the document content is a visual layout discrepancy. This doesn't fundamentally break the document's utility, so it is categorized as a minor defect.
checks.b_evidence_source: The NC DIT logo is positioned in the top-left header area of page 1.
checks.b_evidence_output: The NC DIT logo is rendered as a large image between '3. Executive Director Opening Remarks' and '4. Approval of March Minutes'.
checks.b_evidence_location: Body of document, between Agenda items 3 and 4.

checks.a_analysis: All tables from the source PDF are rendered correctly in the HTML. The 'Committee Members' and 'Guests' tables, which were side-by-side in the PDF, have been merged into a single wide table in the HTML, preserving the horizontal alignment of the data. The 'Staff' table and the vote tables for items 4 and 5 are also correctly rendered with clear headers and aligned row content. X marks indicating attendance and votes are correctly placed.

The primary meaningful image from the source (the agency logo) is present in the HTML output. The 'Approved' watermark from the background of the PDF pages is missing, but this is an acceptable and intended exclusion for accessibility purposes according to the task instructions.

No body content (paragraphs, tables) is duplicated in the HTML output. The appearance of document titles and section headings in both the Table of Contents/Navigation area and the main body is an expected accessibility feature and not considered a defect.
